summary:
Mahmud of Ghazni (reigned 998–1030 AD), sultan of the [[event:2929|Ghaznavid Empire]], conducted seventeen major military campaigns into the Indian subcontinent between 1001 and 1027 AD that transformed the political and religious landscape of northwestern South Asia and established the pattern of Islamic conquest that would eventually result in the Muslim sultanates of Delhi and Bengal. Mahmud's invasions penetrated as far as Gujarat on the western coast and Mathura and Kanauj in the Gangetic plain, sacking some of the wealthiest Hindu temples in India and carrying off enormous quantities of gold, silver, jewels, and elephant ivory.

The most famous of Mahmud's raids was the sack of the Somnath temple in Gujarat (1025 AD), dedicated to the god Shiva, whose legendary wealth — accumulated over centuries of royal patronage — Mahmud looted and whose famous golden idol he allegedly smashed personally. The destruction of Somnath became one of the defining events in Hindu-Muslim historical memory on the subcontinent, endlessly recounted, debated, and politically weaponized over subsequent centuries. Mahmud's campaigns did not aim at permanent conquest and administration of India — he withdrew after each raid, content to extract plunder rather than govern — but they fatally disrupted the Shahi kingdoms of the northwest (Kabul and Punjab), which had previously acted as a buffer between the Islamic world and the Hindu-Buddhist heartland of the subcontinent. His campaigns exposed the vulnerability of Indian states to the mobile cavalry armies of Central Asian origin, foreshadowing the permanent conquests of the Ghurids and Delhi Sultans in the 12th and 13th centuries.
